mode_reason: Pipeline is healthy across all signals. (1) Mode 1: all 8 agents producing on schedule — analyst 2026-04-30T07:51, decider 09:17, curmudgeon 06:36, integrity 08:08, workspace-sync 09:17, poller 23:05 prev day. (2) Mode 2: workspace md5 matches GitHub for all 5 monitored canaries (data/wins.json, data/sections.json, build-scripts/digest-reviews.js, build-scripts/generate-html.js, docs/index.html); project-induced disk footprint 149MB (under 500MB threshold); auth healthy. EXP-277 rescue from yesterday's report has landed (operator pushed build-scripts/generate-html.js + test.js). (3) Mode 4: PROP-013 was implemented yesterday (Part A: never-push-strand- surface in workspace-sync.md is present; Part B: rescue comment fix in decider-patches-and-selfapply.md is MISSING). Most other open PROPs are STILL_BROKEN but stable — verifying without rewriting is the right call this run. Last full Mode 3 run was 2026-04-26 (PROP-009r2 wave). Defaulting to Mode 3 cost engineering per dispatcher rule.

items.issue: PROP-013 (surface NEVER_PUSH strand events to operator)
items.verification: Part A (monitor/integrity/never-push-strand-NNN.json from workspace-sync): grep -q 'never-push-strand-' monitor/prompts/workspace-sync.md → MATCH. Part B (rescue comment fix): grep -q 'NEVER_PUSH does not block cp' monitor/prompts/reference/decider-patches-and-selfapply.md → NO MATCH. Currently in decider-patches-and-selfapply.md line 298: 'WARN: failed to copy $f to FUSE — file may be in NEVER_PUSH deny-list (which is fine; those must be pushed via git directly).' This comment is misleading: NEVER_PUSH controls workspace-sync's PUSH direction, NOT the decider's cp direction. cp from clone→FUSE always succeeds; the file just doesn't get pushed back. Strand files: 0 generated since impl — could mean (a) no NEVER_PUSH strand has occurred since the EXP-277 rescue (most likely; no source-file patches have happened in the 24h since), or (b) the workspace-sync trigger isn't firing. Cannot distinguish without a triggering event.
items.status: PARTIALLY_FIXED — Part A done, Part B not done. Recommendation: operator includes the 5-line comment fix in next clone-push (low-priority; misleading comment doesn't break anything but will confuse the next person reading the rescue path).

STILL_BROKEN — 8 consecutive runs. Recommendation: close wontfix. The decider's queue ops + change-driven cycles already approximate this without an explicit gate; the explicit gate adds dispatcher overhead for marginal benefit.

findings.headline: Pipeline healthy. EXP-277 rescue landed, workspace ↔ git in sync, disk under threshold. Cost-engineering audit reveals (a) workspace-sync.md grew +119 lines (23%) in 24h from PROP-013 Part A implementation — flag as MODERATE bloat; (b) 5 STILL_BROKEN PROPs deserve wontfix decisions to clean the open list; (c) decider's apparent no-op streak in shallow audits was a false alarm caused by deprecated key names — corrected detection shows decider has been productive (5 reviews, 2 patches, 8 issues at 14:05Z).

decider_no_op_false_alarm.observation: Initial Mode 3 audit flagged decider as 7-of-8 no-op based on patches_applied_this_run/issues_created_this_run keys returning empty. These keys are DEPRECATED — current decider reports use self_apply_summary.{patches_applied, issues_created, issues_closed}. Re-running with correct keys: 04-30T14:05 → 5 reviews, 2 patches, 2 closures, 8 opens. 04-29T09:27 → 16 reviews. 04-29T01:22 → 15 reviews. Decider is healthy and productive.
decider_no_op_false_alarm.lesson: Future tinker no-op detection scripts must use self_apply_summary.* and curmudgeon_reviews_processed (a list, not a count field). Updating the cost-engineering reference file's snippets would prevent this false-alarm class.

Adjudicate STILL_BROKEN PROPs: 005, 008, 011, 012. Recommend wontfix for 005, 008, 011; operator decision for 012. Closes 4 stale items in the open queue.

Drain mode reality: 203 open issues vs 50 threshold (4.06x overage). 16 closed in last 7 days vs ongoing inflow. Either (a) bulk-close stale issues, (b) raise threshold to 200, (c) accept current backlog as steady-state and stop alerting on it.

operator_actions_recommended.action: Tinker self-improvement: bundle Mode 1+2 quick-checks into a single script tinker-quickcheck.sh that emits structured JSON. Would reduce sub-agent invocations and standardize the 'is the pipeline healthy?' answer.

other_side_of_the_argument: Counter-argument to the 'STILL_BROKEN PROPs should close wontfix' recommendation: persistent open PROPs ARE the signal that something is wrong. Closing them as wontfix because nobody acts on them is operator-debt-rationalization — it cleans the report at the cost of memory. If PROP-005 (Pre-flight Work Check) were genuinely valuable, the right response is to implement it (operator unable/unwilling) or to write a clear rationale for why the existing decider behavior subsumes it. Just marking wontfix with 'no engagement in 8 runs' as the reason is a soft form of giving up.

Counter-argument to flagging workspace-sync.md +23% growth as MODERATE bloat: the growth was a single justified addition (PROP-013 Part A), and the agent is Haiku — token cost is minimal compared to Opus agents. The bloat-watch heuristic is most valuable for Opus agents whose tokens are 10x more expensive. Applying the same threshold to Haiku agents may be over-engineering for the cost model.

Counter-argument to PROP-013 partial-implementation framing: Part A (the surfacing mechanism in workspace-sync.md) is the load-bearing piece. Part B (the rescue comment fix) is documentation polish. Calling this 'PARTIALLY_FIXED' implies more remaining work than is truly required. The proposal's value is delivered. Simpler framing: 'PROP-013 implemented; small comment polish recommended.'

Counter-argument to schedule-table fix urgency (priority 1): operator has chosen not to fix this for 5 consecutive runs. That is itself information — either the drift doesn't actually hurt anything, or the operator weighs the work as low-value. Tinker reflexively re-asking is worse than re-asking; it normalizes ignoring the recommendation. A more useful move: stop reporting it as P1 and instead include a single-line 'doc drift unchanged' note. Reserve P1 for things actually load-bearing.

Counter-argument to the 'no-op false alarm' lesson: the cost-engineering reference snippets that produced the false alarm are STILL using deprecated keys. Updating them is a self-fix candidate. But the reference file is NEVER_PUSH so it can't be self-fixed. Recommendation: bundle the snippet update with priority 1 (schedule fix) in the next operator clone-push.

Counter-argument to drain-mode HNOTE's instruction 'do NOT re-flag this as P1 mismatch': blanket 'do not re-flag' instructions create blind spots. If the drain mode persists indefinitely (203 open vs 50 threshold, no clear path to drain), the priority-queue.json schedule_state field will lie indefinitely. At some point, either drain mode should be acknowledged as a permanent state (and the field updated to match) or the threshold should be raised. The current pattern is the worst of both worlds: the field is wrong, but we're told not to flag it.
